channel.title: 406mtsports.com - RSS Results of type article
channel.description: 406mtsports.com - RSS Results only for asset type(s) of article

item.title: Montana Grizzlies add Texas commit to 2027 high school recruiting class
item.description:
MISSOULA — The Montana football team got its second-known out-of-state commit and its first player from Texas in the 2027 recruiting class.

Rye Roberts became the ninth-known overall commit in head coach Bobby Kennedy's first recruiting class when he shared his pledge on social media Sunday. His commitment graphic listed him at the "athlete" position.

He chose the Grizzlies over offers from FBS teams Army and Air Force. He had also received interest from FBS team Boise State. He got his UM offer when he visited campus for Junior Day on March 6.

"I'm thankful to announce my commitment to the Montana Grizzlies," he wrote in his social media post. "Thank you for all the support from my mom and dad and the rest of my family who have provided for me and been by my side every step of the way. Thank you for all the coaches that have helped develop me into who I am today, and thank you #montanagrizzlies and the coaches that have given me this opportunity. Most importantly ALL GLORY to God."

Roberts, who is listed at 5-foot-10 and 180 pounds, has played wide receiver and running back on offense and cornerback on defense in high school at New Braunfels Christian Academy, which is located just outside of San Antonio, Texas.

He earned second-team all-state honors at both a wide receiver and defensive back in TAPPS Division III, the third largest of four enrollment-based divisions in the Texas Association of Private and Parochial Schools. He also got first-team all-state honors as a special teams returner and a defensive back in the Padilla Coaches Poll.

He caught 43 passes for 732 yards and 12 touchdowns in 10 games this past season, according to MaxPreps. He also carried the ball three times for 65 yards and one score. Defensively, he totaled 58 tackles, eight interceptions, one pick-6 and one forced fumble. He returned two punts and one kickoff for touchdowns and blocked three field goals/extra points.

Also a track and field athlete, he won the TAPPS Class 3A state championship in the triple jump at 44 feet, 9.25 inches in spring 2025 as a sophomore. He placed seventh in the long jump and ran the first leg on the 4x200 relay team that finished sixth.

Roberts joins a Grizzlies recruiting class that includes Missoula Sentinel linebacker Sam Sirmon, Missoula Big Sky defensive lineman Quincy King and athlete Eli Kasberg, Kalispell Glacier wide receiver Cooper Pelc, defensive end Frank Kapohai Abreu from Hawaii, Helena Capital wide receiver Drew Almquist, Billings West athlete Jack Ryan and Manhattan wide receiver Brayden Zikmund.

media:content.media:title: Montana Grizzlies Rye Roberts
media:content.media:description: Rye Roberts holds a Grizzlies football on a visit to campus in March. The Texas native committed to Montana in the 2027 recruiting class.

media:content.media:title: Montana Grizzlies head coach Bobby Kennedy on spring football game

item.title: MSU-bound Frenchtown thrower sets shot put, discus meet records at Top Ten
item.description:
MISSOULA — An overcast, damp Tuesday afternoon at Missoula County Stadium had once again been sprayed with smatterings of rain as the boys shot put finals commenced.

Frenchtown senior Henry Griffin had been flirting with a 60-foot throw throughout the preliminaries, recording heaves of 58 feet and seven inches and 59-3.5. His second to last throw came as rain continued to trickle down, a mat of cloth laid for the throwers to dry their shoes on before their turns.

The 6-foot-4, 225-pound Bronc wound up, pirouetted on his toes and launched the ball through the misty air. Fans murmured as the ball landed. It appeared right in the range of 60 feet.

The announced distance? 59 feet, nine and a half inches.

"That's rage bait," Griffin joked to himself as he heard the number.

The distance was still a personal-best for Griffin, though, and it set a new meet record at the Russ Pilcher Top Ten meet. His throw eclipsed Justin Jenks (Helena Capital) by one foot and four and a half inches, and it also set a new school record at Frenchtown.

Add to that a discus throw of 186-2 that not only beat out the second-place finisher by 30 feet but set a new meet record by over four feet — a school record as well — and Tuesday marked another quality day at the office for Griffin.

The senior won a state championship in football in the fall, secured a trophy at the state tournament with the Broncs basketball team this winter and is now hoping for more hardware in the spring before beginning his track and field career with the Montana State Bobcats. A change in his mental approach, Griffin told 406 MT Sports, has helped spark a dominant season.

"I'm just searching for consistency in my marks," he said. "Consistency causes breakthroughs. When you worry about a mark, it just gets in your head a little bit."

Griffin's athletic resume at Frenchtown is the definition of a well-rounded athlete. Starting football for the Broncs as a junior after golfing for his first two years of high school, he was named all-conference honorable mention as a junior offensive lineman. Then, as a senior, he was named Southwestern A defensive MVP with 18 TFL's and 66 total tackles. He helped Frenchtown capture its first state title since 2009 in a jaw-dropping classic against Billings Central at Frenchtown High.

Then, as a contributor on the boys basketball team, Griffin helped Frenchtown secure a third-place trophy, its first trophy at the state tournament in recent memory. Griffin also begged to join the Broncs baseball team, he said, but was unable to make it work with track season. He has also played soccer.

"I did all of the things," Griffin joked.

The Bronc started throwing in middle school and his lineage is a big reason why. His aunt, Lorna Griffin, was an Olympic thrower from Corvallis who earned silver medals in the shot put and discus throws at the 1983 Pan-American games. She was selected for the 1980 U.S. Olympic team but was unable to compete due to the U.S. boycott of the 1980 games in Moscow, but went on to compete in 1984 in Los Angeles.

Nevertheless, she would train with Henry every Sunday for years and work on his abilities.

"He comes from a family of throwers," Frenchtown throwers coach Archie Petritz said. "I knew he was always going to have a knack for it."

Griffin placed sixth at the Class A state meet in the discus as a freshman, registering a launch of 143-2. As a sophomore, he was up to fifth in the discus and 14th in shot put. Last season, as a junior, he placed second in the discus and third in shot put.

The development and growth shown, Petritz said, comes from a mix of technique and strength training.

"His mentality has never changed," Petritz added. "It's just, 'How can I get better each week?'"

The rise up the leaderboards also brought attention from the college recruitment world. Griffin said he never knew what recruitment would be like, but quickly realized that, for track athletes, the onus is on the athlete in many cases to reach out to schools.

Attending camps around the area like in Corvallis and in Coeur d'Alene helped him get seen and watched by college programs. From there, Griffin went to work firing off emails. Once he had earned an offer from Montana State, the decision was decided for the lifelong Bobcat fan.

Griffin spent his early years growing up in Twin Bridges and got a puppy when he was four years old. He named him Cody Kirk, after the Montana State running back who totaled 48 touchdowns for the Cats. Kirk also happened to be a Frenchtown native.

"When we moved to Frenchtown, it was a little weird that we had a dog named Cody Kirk," Griffin recalled.

Griffin received interest from colleges for football, too, notably Montana Tech and Carroll College, but a self-described hard decision came down to academics. MSU's strong engineering program proved to be the difference.

Before he heads east to Gallatin County, Griffin hopes to polish off one of the most successful senior years an athlete can enjoy with another pair of state titles in his two events. He's not finished in any spot but first in either event all season.

However, as he's warming up before the discus throw — shaking out his arms, jogging across the field, bouncing around to stay loose — he's not just competing with his opponents or with his personal standards. He's competing for the Griffins to come.

His sixth-grade younger brother, Holden, will also be throwing. Henry wants to make his records as tough to beat as possible.

"It's kind of selfish, but I just want to make it harder on my little brother," Henry said.

item.title: Cat-Griz Insider Podcast: Spring ball ends, NFL opportunities extended
item.description: 406 MT Sports' Victor Flores begins the latest episode of the Cat-Griz Insider Podcast with some thoughts on the Montana State football team's spring game. Then he talks with Frank Gogola about the former Bobcats and Montana Grizzlies who received NFL opportunities (17:32), UM's latest football commits (22:38), MSU's Norah Seidl winning the Big Sky women's golf individual title (25:35), the Griz softball team's end to the regular season (27:12), and the defensive linemen, linebackers, defensive backs and special teams for the 2026 Cats and Griz (30:08).

item.title: 'No magic other than effort': Defense has keyed Missoula Sentinel's perfect start
item.description:
HELENA — As the Missoula Sentinel Spartans nursed a one-run lead against Helena Capital on Tuesday, the wind, which swirled all day, started blowing to left field.

It's not the ideal scenario, at least not for the defense. Yet, the Spartans have been navigating those with success all season and did so again on their way to a 13-0 start that included a 3-2 victory over the Bruins.

If a ball off the bat of Capital junior Gretta Canney, a two-out fly ball to left, had carried just a few feet farther, the outcome could have been different.

As soon as Canney made contact, Piper Whitman, who was on to close out the game for Sentinel, was concerned.

"Yea, I was worried," she said. "I had complete confidence in Alyssa (Hanninen) though. I turned around and knew she would come down with it."

That confidence was well placed. Hanninen, a pitcher and outfielder just like Whitman, made the over-the-shoulder catch to put the win on ice, keeping the perfect season intact.

"I actually thought to myself beforehand, I feel like it's going to be hit to me," Hanninen said. "I knew it was deep and I heard everybody yelling...I knew it wasn't getting down. I was happy that we got the last out."

The seventh inning was actually a 1-2-3 affair. It couldn't even be called a jam, not like the one Spartans got into in the bottom of the fifth, when the Bruins, trailing by one, had runners on second and third with no outs.

Whitman came through with a strikeout to get the first out. Then, the senior forced a pop out that turned into a double play, allowing Sentinel to get out of the inning.

"We rely on our defense a lot this year, and that's what winning us our games" Hanninen said. "That's with everybody. One person has a game and another person has a game, but we all seem to be gelling really well."

Defense has certainly been the calling card for Sentinel, which is just fifth in the Western AA in runs scored (49). The Spartans have allowed just 20 runs total in 13 games (1.53 per game) on their way to building a one-game lead in the division over Glacier (7-1) and Missoula Big Sky (5-2). No other team in the conference has allowed fewer than 35.

"We all have trust in each other," Whitman said. "We read each other very well. We know where our next play is going and our communication has been really good this year and that helps to have us all on the same page."

"The good thing is the girls never seem to believe they have lost," Sentinel head coach Wade Sellers said. "They've got the grit and just find a way. It's not always pretty, but they keep doing it. There's no magic other than effort."

On Tuesday at Mike Miller Fields at Northwest Park in Helena, the Spartans faced some early adversity in their third straight one-run victory as Madi Emmert drove the first pitch, at least in the bottom half of the first inning, right out of the ballpark to put the Bruins up 1-0.

The Capital lead didn't last for long though. Baily Bridger blasted a solo home run of her own to even the score on the first pitch of the second inning.

In the third, Hanninen aided the winning effort at the plate with a double that turned into a run thanks to an RBI single from Whitman. Sentinel plated another run, on a sac fly in the fourth, before Seeley Schwenk answered with an RBI single in the fifth to get the Bruins back within one.

"We're starting to find our groove a little bit," Capital head coach Ashten Broadhead said. "I saw a lot of confidence and maturity in this game. I'm really proud."

The Bruins went toe-to-toe with the top team in the Western AA, which was an improvement from earlier in the season.

"We had Glacier up 5-2 and just kind of unraveled," Broadhead said. "It really showed today that we can play with anybody that we want to play with as long as we play together."

The Bruins lost that game to the Wolfpack, the defending state champions, 16-6. That's one of seven defeats for CHS (4-7, 2-4). Capital is currently sixth in the Western AA.

Emmert and Canney paced the Bruins with two hits each. Schwenk had the other hit and an RBI. Calyse Hendrix had one of her best outings of the season in defeat, going seven innings in the circle, while giving up just two earned runs on six hits. She struck out six and walked zero.

Whitman got the win for the Spartans after giving up just one run in four innings of work. She was credited with five strikeouts and led the offense with two hits.

"It's been great," she said of the 13-0 start. "It's been fun all around."

The Spartans will be back in Helena on Thursday to take on the Helena High Bengals, who defeated Missoula Hellgate 11-1 on Tuesday in Missoula. Capital will host Hellgate.

media:content.media:title: 042826-ir-spt-softball-Sentinel-6.jpg
media:content.media:description: Misssoula Sentinel's Piper Whitman throws out the Helena Capital runner at first Tuesday at Mike Miller Field.

media:content.media:title: 042826-ir-spt-softball-Sentinel-1.jpg
media:content.media:description: Missoula Sentinel's Alyssa Hanninen connects with a pitch from Helena Capital Tuesday at Mike Miller Field.

media:content.media:title: 042826-ir-spt-softball-CHS-16.jpg
media:content.media:description: Helena Capital's Calyse Hendrix reacts to striking out a Missoula Sentinel batter Tuesday at Mike Miller Field.

item.title: Montana State's Matt Logie excited to retain every scholarship player entering 4th season
item.description:
The NCAA Division I men’s basketball transfer portal window was open April 7-21, but Montana State had a solid grasp of its roster well before then.

The program announced on March 27, just 19 days after its season ended at the Big Sky Conference tournament in Boise, Idaho, that the team would return intact save for the four graduating seniors Jed Miller, Patrick McMahon, Chris Hodges and Davian Brown. Walk-on player Grayson Gaddis would ultimately join that group, choosing to attend a school closer to his home in Utah.

Aside from that quintet, the Bobcats will have a very familiar makeup in 2026-27. They will return every scholarship player with eligibility remaining — Christian King, Jeremiah Davis, Jaden Steppe, Seth Amunrud, Howie Keene, CJ Purdie, Waka Mbatch, Cavin Holden and James Steward — plus walk-on Calum Rutherfurd.

Logie said the team met in the days after the loss to Idaho in the conference tournament, and he communicated to his players — including six who have already transferred once in their careers — how hopeful he was that they would want to continue building on last season’s success. His message was well-received, both then and later in individual meetings with the players.

“Because of the adversity that this last year's team faced, we have a hungry group of players and coaches that feel like there's some unfinished opportunities out there for us,” Logie told the Chronicle on April 10, “and I think collectively we all realized that the fastest path to make some of those dreams come true is by circling the wagons and sticking together.”

Retaining essentially the whole roster should be considered a tremendous offseason victory for a program of MSU’s size, Logie said. And doing so made this year’s transfer portal experience far easier than in the past.

“The clarity of our picture is vastly different than what many programs — and we've been in that situation at this time in previous years — can communicate right now,” Logie said. “I think we're one of the very few programs at this level currently that can bring a young man into the locker room and talk about exactly who his teammates will be, exactly what opportunities (will be available).”

Director of Athletics Leon Costello praised the Bobcat Collective, the 6th Man Club and the athletic department as a whole for their collective effort in keeping the bulk of Logie’s winningest MSU team to date in Bozeman.

“All three of those organizations have contributed extra dollars to make sure that the budgets and things in order to operate are where they need to be,” Costello said. “We're in the business of retaining coaches, and players as well, and so we have to make sure that we put our best foot forward, to make sure the finances and funding and support is in place for all of our coaches to be able to keep the student-athletes that they recruit.”

That investment has already shown to be worth it, Costello said.

“You need everything around you for these student-athletes to believe that this is the best and right spot for them, and seeing what we saw at the end of this year was pretty special,” Costello said. “And I'm not surprised at all. I'm happy for Matt, I'm happy for the program, that we get to have more time and more continuity on this team because I really believe this (upcoming) year that's going to produce some really great results.”

Logie also credited the Bobcat Collective and the 6th Man Club for their assistance in helping “our basketball program to compete at the highest level within the Big Sky.”

“That certainly takes a lot of different people pulling in the same direction,” Logie added.

Logie was thankful also for the community support he has seen in his three years at MSU, citing the Bobcats’ league-leading attendance in each of the last two years (an average of 2,976 fans in 2025-26 and 3,021 the year before).

“All of those things play a factor,” he said, “along with just the experience that our guys have in this community, being Bobcat athletes and being made to have those experiences be so special and so joyful.”

Early look at 2026-27 schedule

According to the documents available in Montana State’s public records disclosure, MSU’s men have four games already set for next season.

Two of those contests are the return meetings outlined in prior home-and-home contracts. After playing at Long Beach State and at Cal Poly in November and December, respectively, last season, the Bobcats will host LBSU on Nov. 7 and Cal Poly on Nov. 15 this year.

The two other game contracts currently available are for a pair of guarantee games, contests in which MSU receives payment for participating. Those games are Nov. 12 at Big 12 team BYU in Provo, Utah, and Dec. 11 at new Pac-12 team San Diego State. MSU will receive $100,000 for playing BYU and $90,000 for playing SDSU, per the respective game contracts.

Logie’s first Montana State team played three guarantee games in the 2023-24 season (Seattle, Cal and Washington) and have played five in each of the last two seasons: Wisconsin, Wichita State, Northwestern, USC and TCU in 2024-25 and Colorado, Stanford, Boise State, Utah State and Oregon State in 2025-26.

Logie said he’s hopeful that his program can again arrange four or five guarantee games.

“We're in the space where a lot of conversations and verbal agreements have been made and we’re waiting for contracts to get signed,” he said. “We may not have five of those, but I don’t think we’ll have less than four.”

Logie said those games against teams from the power conferences help prepare his players for Big Sky play and also give them experience against such teams once the postseason arrives. To his point, last season’s Bobcats lost those five guarantee games by an average of 6.4 points (including one game in overtime) and went on to post the most overall wins (18) and Big Sky wins (12) in Logie’s tenure.

“You need to have some game reps and some experience playing against teams like that if you are ever going to have that one shining moment, March Madness victory,” Logie said. “I think being exposed to those things in November and December allow you to learn some lessons and figure out how to prepare for when the time comes in March.”

media:content.media:title: Montana State's Matt Logie, Jed Miller, Patrick McMahon on loss to Idaho
media:content.media:description: From left, Montana State head men's basketball coach Matt Logie, senior guard Jed Miller and senior forward Patrick McMahon talk to reporters after their 78-74 loss to Idaho in the quarterfinals of the Big Sky Conference tournament Sunday, March 8, 2026, at Idaho Central Arena in Boise, Idaho.

item.title: Wyoming all-star basketball rosters for series with Montana released
item.description:
BILLINGS — The Wyoming rosters for the annual Montana-Wyoming All-Star Basketball Series June 12-13 have been released.

The Wyoming boys team features four players who have committed to continue their basketball careers at the collegiate level with additional college commitments expected in the near future, per a press release on the Gillette (Wyoming) College athletics website.

Among the college commitments on the boys team are Nate Miner of Sheridan, who will be playing basketball at Rocky Mountain College per the Wyoming news release. Also on that list is Jack Andela of Gillette Campbell County, who committed to Carroll; Cody Bomengen of Thermopolis, who has committed to Gillette College and Collin Roberts of Douglas, who has committed to Northwest College.

Per the news release, the complete Wyoming boys all-stars with honors are as follows: Carter Alvar, Casper Kelly Walsh, 6-1 guard, first team 4A all-state; Jack Andela, Gillette Campbell County, 6-8 forward, first team two-time 4A all-state (Carroll College); Cody Bomengen, Thermopolis, 6-6 forward, two-time 3A all-state (Gillette College); Trypp Burtsfield, Gillette Thunder Basin, 6-0 guard; Mason Eager, Casper Kelly Walsh, 6-3 guard, 4A all-state; Cooper Lancaster, Star Valley, 6-5 guard, two-time 4A all-state; Nate Miner, Sheridan, 6-5 guard, two-time 4A all-state (Rocky Mountain College); Gavin Patik, Casper Natrona County, 6-4 forward, 4A all-conference; Collin Roberts, Douglas, 6-foot guard, two-time 3A all-state (Northwest College); and Owen Walker, Lovell, 6-6 forward, two-time 3A all-state.

The Wyoming boys head coach is Shawn Neary. Neary is the head men’s basketball coach at Gillette College. The assistant coaches are Brendan Howard, Randy Hite, and Matt Mordecai.

Howard, an assistant at Gillette College, is from Great Falls and had collegiate stops at Eastern Washington, Montana State Billings, North Dakota and the University of Providence. Howard helped Montana to a 2-0 sweep against Wyoming in 2017.

The Montana rosters were released earlier this month.

The Montana boys swept Wyoming last year and increased their lead in the all-time series to 69-29.

The Wyoming girls team features two players who will play both collegiate volleyball and basketball in Jaylin Mills, Sundance, Northwest College and Chaney Reish, Tongue River, Northwest College. Erica Wilson of Pinedale will play collegiate basketball at Northwest College. Elizabeth Needham of Cheyenne Central will play basketball at Laramie County Community College. Sydney Simone of Cody has committed to play volleyball at Carroll College.

Per the Gillette College news release, the complete Wyoming girls all-stars are as follows: Jaylin Mills, Sundance, committed to play basketball and volleyball at Northwest College; Chaney Reish, Tongue River, committed to play basketball and volleyball at Northwest College; Erica Wilson, Pinedale, committed to play basketball at Northwest College; Elizabeth Needham, Cheyenne Central, committed to play basketball at Laramie County Community College; Cashlynn Haws, Cheyenne East, will serve a mission in Tacloban, Philippines; Camryn Wagner, Sheridan, attend University of Utah; Reece McGrath, Gillette Thunder Basin, attend University of Wyoming; Ashtyn Ketchum, Gillette Thunder Basin, attend University of Wyoming; Addy Rouse, Gillette Thunder Basin, attend Nova Southeastern University; Sydney Simone, Cody, committed to volleyball at Carroll College.

Liz Lewis, the head women’s basketball coach at Gillette College, is once again the Wyoming head coach. Assistants coaches are Lou Jenkins and Shelby Kline, both of whom are assistants at Gillette College.

Last year at Lockwood in the second game of the series, the Wyoming girls snapped a 16-game winning streak by Montana.

Montana now leads the all-time series 42-14.

This year’s games are Friday, June 12, at the Pronghorn Center on the Gillette College campus and Saturday, June 13, at the Lockwood High School gym. The girls are slated to start at 5 p.m. and the boys 7 p.m. both nights.

The boys series will celebrate its 50th anniversary this year, while the girls series began in 1997.

Per the Gillette College news release, plans call for a special recognition of the historic 50th anniversary milestone. The press released stated that organizers are preparing a series of events to celebrate the rivalry.

media:content.media:title: Montana-Wyoming All-Stars boys basketball
media:content.media:description: The Wyoming All-Star team huddles up during the Montana-Wyoming All-Star boys basketball game at Lockwood High School on Saturday, June 14, 2025.

media:content.media:title: Montana-Wyoming All-Stars girls basketball
media:content.media:description: A young fan holds up a sign for the Wyoming All-Star's Elyn Bowers during the Montana-Wyoming All-Star girls basketball game at Lockwood High School on Saturday, June 14, 2025.

media:content.media:title: 2025 Montana-Wyoming All-Star Series
media:content.media:description: The Montana boys swept Wyoming and the girls split the 2025 series during the Midland Roundtable Montana-Wyoming All-Star basketball games June 14, 2025 at Lockwood High School.

item.title: 2026 Montana track and field bests (April 28)
item.description:
High school track and field

Top 10 rankings

Lists compiled from meet results submitted on athletic.net. State record times, which can only be set at the state meet, listed in parentheses.

(Through April 28)

BOYS

100 meters (10.5): Cooper Nelson, Helena Capital, 10.49; Brayden Brisko, Helena Capital, 10.75; William Hollensteiner, Kalispell Flathead, 10.81; Ian Bullard, Missoula Hellgate, 10.92; Garret Prather, Columbus, 10.95; Wesley Ehret, Belgrade, 10.99; Brayden Zikmund, Manhattan, 11.0; Reed Joseph Finneman, Missoula Sentinel, 11.01; Hunter Cadena, Missoula Sentinel, 11.01; Robert Merchant, Bigfork, 11.02.

AA-Nelson, Helena Capital; A-Merchant, Bigfork; B-Prather, Columbus; C-Dawson Cook, Belt, 11.36.

200 meters (21.06): Garret Prather, Columbus, 21.88; William Hollensteiner, Kalispell Flathead, 21.92; Elijah Gibson, Billings West, 22.19; Cooper Nelson, Helena Capital, 22.28; Robert Merchant, Bigfork, 22.29; Dawson Hammond, Malta, 22.36; Ryland Humphrey, Billings West, 22.41; Cooper Pelc, Kalispell Glacier, 22.43; Cameron Leprowse, Missoula Sentinel, 22.44; Preston Welch, Shepherd, 22.51.

AA-Hollensteiner, Kalispell Flathead; A-Merchant, Bigfork; B-Prater, Columbus; C-Jacob Swanz, Denton-Geyser-Stanford, 23.33.

400 meters (47.22): William Hollensteiner, Kalispell Flathead, 47.72; Ledger Martin, Conrad, 49.91; McKay Shobe, Lewistown, 50.26; Blake Williams, Missoula Big Sky, 50.29; Preston Welch, Shepherd, 50.33; Colter Anderson, Big Timber, 50.36; Josh Lambertsen, Eureka, 50.52; Gage Wardell, Belgrade, 50.63; Dawson Hammond, Malta, 50.74; Brett Chivers, Kalispell Flathead, 50.9.

AA-Hollensteiner, Kalispell Flathead; A-Shobe, Lewistown; B-Martin, Conrad; C-Danny Blanchet, Power-Dutton-Brady, 51.22.

800 meters (1:50.21): Reid Bartholomew, Bozeman Gallatin, 1:53.55^(1:56.67); Taylor Neil, Bozeman, 1:56.6; Owen Thiel, Kalispell Glacier, 1:57.21; Dylan Hill, Helena, 1:57.23; Miles Halvorsen, Bozeman, 1:57.44; Christian Landers, Bozeman, 1:57.5; Finn Schretenthaler, Livingston, 1:58.04; Milo Kauffman, Helena, 1:58.26; Ben Bird, Hardin, 1:58.57; Axel Kowalski, Helena, 1:58.79.

AA-Bartholomew, Bozeman Gallatin; A-Schretenthaler, Livingston; B-Auston Schellig, Joliet, 1:58.8; C-Kaiden Holmes, Bainville, 2:06.3# (2:07.23).

^Ran time at Arcadia Invitational in California, () best time while in Montana

#Ran time at Watford City Early Bird in North Dakota, () best time while in Montana

1,600 meters (4:04.89): Owen Thiel, Kalispell Glacier, 4:08.85#(4:25.95); Miles Halvorsen, Bozeman, 4:13.23^(); Taylor Neil, Bozeman, 4:14.11^(); Grey Piseno, Billings Central, 4:15.37%(); David DiGiallonardo, Polson, 4:16.79^(); Branlin Foote, Bozeman, 4:18.81^(); John Jermyn, Plains, 4:25.44; Cord Greer, Plains, 4:25.56; Wilson Schmidt, Belgrade, 4:25.65; Finn Schretenthaler, Livingston, 4:25.65.

AA-Thiel, Kalispell Glacier; A-Piseno, Billings Central; B-Jermyn, Plains; C-Trent Lane, Cascade, 4:39.64.

^Ran time at Arcadia Invitational in California, () best time while in Montana

item.title: 2 Columbia Falls track and field standouts earn weekly Frontier Conference honors
item.description:
Montana Western and Montana Tech received the sixth edition of the 2026 Erck Hotels/Frontier Conference Men’s Outdoor Track & Filed weekly honors, announced by the conference office Tuesday.

Men’s Outdoor Field Athlete-of-the-Week: Stanley Stremick, Montana Western

Stremick was the top NAIA place finisher in the long jump for Montana Western at the NCAA Division I Montana Open. He jumped 6.84 meters (22 feet, 5.25 inches) to place fourth behind three NCAA Division I long jumpers.

Stremick also jumped 12.77 meters (41 feet, 10.75 inches) in the triple jump for the Bulldogs.

Men's Outdoor Track Athlete-of-the-Week: Adam Schrader, Montana Tech

Schrader earned his second career NAIA ‘B’ standard in the 400-meter hurdles in the NCAA Division I Montana Open for Montana Tech. He covered the distance in a career-best 53.32 seconds. His time ranks 21st in the NAIA and second all-time at Tech.

Schrader also led off the Orediggers’ 4x400-meter relay team, joining teammates Morgan McClernan, Konnor Gregerson and Matthew Moreni to win in a season-best time of 3:18.34 (3:18.78 converted for altitude).

This is Schrader's first career Erck Hotels/Frontier Conference Men's Outdoor Track & Field weekly honor.

media:content.media:title: Stanley Stremick, Adam Schrader Frontier Conference POTW
media:content.media:description: Montana Western's Stanley Stremick and Montana Tech's Adam Schrader earned Frontier Conference weekly track and field accolades Tuesday.

item.title: 'Earn it the hard way': Carroll College OT Andrew Devine receives NFL tryout
item.description:
HELENA — Alex Hoffman’s undrafted free agent opportunity with the NFL’s New Orleans Saints in 2021 helped influence Andrew Devine’s Carroll College commitment. Like Hoffman, a former high school quarterback, Devine entered college undersized for an offensive tackle – 210 pounds hanging off a 6-foot-8 basketball-playing frame.

Sunday, the now 320-pound Devine received a phone call confirming invitation to Kansas City Chiefs Rookie Minicamp. The 2025 Frontier Conference West Division first-team left tackle will leave his hometown of Wasilla, Alaska Thursday morning and fly to Missouri, where a three-day tryout to achieve Devine’s professional playing dream awaits.

“I was pretty damn stoked, very excited,” Devine said. “Really excited for an opportunity to compete. That was my big thing, I just wasn’t sure if I was going to get an opportunity. Now that I have that opportunity, that’s all I really wanted. Now I’ll go earn it the hard way.”

Devine, a two-starter for the Carroll Saints, participated in Montana State’s NFL Pro Day earlier this month. He banged out 26 bench press reps and vertical jumped 29 inches. Devine’s 40-yard dash clocked 5.21 seconds and his pro shuttle 4.71 seconds. Devine’s wingspan measured 80.5 inches, his arms 33 ⅛ inches, and hands 9 ⅝ inches.

Immediately following that workout, Devine didn’t hear any news. A week ago, in the days leading up to the NFL Draft, Devine received text messages and a phone call from the Chiefs. A scout told Devine to keep his cell phone on and be ready to go. Friday, another Kansas City scout called, this one after having talked to Devine’s offensive line coach, Luke Hyde.

Anticipation filled Devine’s childhood home in Wasilla. Surrounded by family, including seven siblings, four of which are married, have children and all live within 30 minutes of where they grew up, Devine patiently waited for an opportunity that seemed distant only two years ago.

“I thought I was ready to be done with football after this season,” Devine said. “We got to that [Montana] Tech [playoff] game and it was a pretty disappointing loss. I thought we were going to win a national championship.

“I felt like, ‘holy crap, I’m just starting to get good. I’m just starting to figure it out. I feel like I can get so much better. My ceiling has not been fulfilled.’ I took a couple days, prayed about it, and I’m like, ‘this is what I want to do.’”

On Monday, Devine chatted with Pittsburgh Steelers offensive lineman Dylan Cook. A Butte High graduate and former quarterback, Cook played for MSU-Northern before transferring to the University of Montana. His professional career began with Tampa Bay in 2022 and continued with Pittsburgh until Cook made his NFL debut last season.

Cook reminded Devine to be confident, aggressive, and to hold nothing back. Cook and former Butte High Bulldog and Montana State quarterback Tommy Mellott are insured by State Farm agent Mike Paffhausen, according to Devine. Paffhausen is a former Carroll football player and helped hook Devine up with his current agent Chris Murray.

When Devine decided last December to pursue professional football, he Googled pre-draft offensive line training facilities. Devine settled on Triple F Elite Sports Training in Knoxville, Tennessee. He emailed Lee Smith, a former NFL player who heads the company’s combine training. Smith responded the next day, and on Dec. 26, Devine flew to the Volunteer State.

Three months of film, weight training, speed and agility drills, and on-field workouts followed. Far from home, Devine enjoyed southern hospitality while his body changed and skills enhanced.

“My first few days there, I was like, ‘what am I doing? I’m in a whole new world. I’m trying to play professional football,’” Devine said. “Every day I got a little bit better, a little more confident. I was like, ‘I do belong, I have a chance at this.’

“I feel like I’ve improved drastically as a player. Got a lot more athletic and a lot more confident.”

Chiefs rookie minicamp begins Friday. It’s a three-day unpadded event, Devine said, filled with film sessions and on-field work. The Kansas City Star currently lists 13 additional minicamp invitees including Montana cornerback Kenzel Lawler. University of Pittsburgh guard Jeff Persi is the only other offensive line invitee, according to the publication.

“Showing up, showing them my physicality,” Devine said. “The strength, the speed, but then the control. Being skilled, being really attentive in the meetings and being able to be a fast learner.”

Devine graduated from Carroll last fall with a philosophy degree and history minor. If Devine had chosen not to pursue professional football, seminary school was likely his next stop, he said.

“God has given me some gifts that I want to use to the best of my ability,” Devine said. “I’m going to see it through to the end. If I don’t, I’m going to be laying in my bed one night and I’m not going to be able to sleep because I’m going to be wondering ‘what if?’ What if I would have tried?

“If I try my best and fail, I can live with that. If I don’t try, I don’t know if I can live with myself.”

Devine is the first known Carroll Saint to either become an undrafted free agent signee or rookie minicamp invitee since Hoffman in 2021. Former Carroll defensive lineman Hunter Peck will attend Denver Broncs camp. Peck earned Frontier Defensive MVP and NAIA All-America honors with the Saints before transferring to Montana. Peck is a Colorado native, but his father is originally from Bigfork.

Former Helena High and Montana Tech defensive lineman Keyshawn James-Newby was drafted in the seventh round by the Philadelphia Eagles Saturday. James-Newby was an All-Frontier selection in 2022.

media:content.media:title: 110825-ir-spt-football-CC-3.jpg
media:content.media:description: Carroll College OT Andrew Devine (#70) battles with College of Idaho defensive lineman Jacob Sisson (#16) during the Yotes' 36-13 victory Saturday, Nov. 8, 2025, inside Nelson Stadium.

media:content.media:title: 111624-406spt-techfb-006.jpg
media:content.media:description: Carroll College offensive lineman Andrew Devine (70) takes the field with the Fighting Saints during the Frontier Conference football game between Montana Tech and Carroll College on Saturday, Nov. 16, 2024 at Alumni Coliseum.

media:content.media:title: Andrew Devine and Ben Paz
media:content.media:description: Carroll College offensive linemen Andrew Devine and Ben Paz attempt to stop the pass rush during the Saints' spring game on Saturday, April 23, 2022, inside Nelson Stadium.

media:content.media:title: Carroll's Andrew Devine on pro day
media:content.media:description: Former Carroll offensive lineman Andrew Devine talks to reporters after participating in Montana State's pro day Thursday, April 2, 2026, at the Kennedy-Stark Athletic Center in Bozeman.

item.title: Carroll College's Claire Bucklin earns weekly Frontier Conference accolade
item.description:
Dakota State and Carroll Carroll received 2026 Erck Hotels/Frontier Conference Women’s Outdoor Track & Field weekly honors for the second straight week.

The sixth edition of the weekly awards were announced by the conference office Tuesday.

Women’s Outdoor Field Athlete-of-the-Week: Amelie Steins, Dakota State

Steins achieved her career and heptathlon record 4,796 points to win the 2026 Frontier Conference Women’s Outdoor Championship. She is currently No. 5 in the NAIA heptathlon standings with her NAIA ‘A’ automatic qualifying mark.

Steins broke the previous school record last season with 4,375 points. This is Steins’ second career Erck Hotels/Frontier Conference Women’s Outdoor Track & Field weekly honor.

Women’s Outdoor Track Athlete-of-the-Week: Claire Bucklin, Carroll College

Bucklin set her personal record in the 100-meter hurdles for Carroll at the NCAA Division I Montana Open. She clocked a time of 15 seconds.

She was also part of the Saints’ 4x400-meter relay team, capturing the team title with a time of 3 minutes, 53.75 seconds (3:54.19 converted for altitude). Bucklin teamed up with Chloe Raats, Katelyn Christensen, and Madalen Shipman to produce the team’s season best time in the 4x400-meter relay.

This is Bucklin’s second career Erck Hotels/Frontier Conference Women’s Outdoor Track & Field weekly honor.

media:content.media:title: Amelie Steins, Claire Bucklin Frontier Conference POTW
media:content.media:description: Dakota State's Amelie Steins and Carroll College's Claire Bucklin earned Frontier Conference weekly track and field accolades Tuesday.
